courses.name: AI and Transformation Management
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to fundamental artificial intelligence (AI) technologies and their applications in organizations, examine how AI drives organizational transformation and change management, develop students' ability to analyze, plan, and implement AI-driven initiatives, explore the ethical, social, and operational challenges of AI adoption, enhance critical thinking and problem-solving skills in AI-enabled business contexts, and prepare students to communicate AI strategies and transformation plans effectively.
courses.description: This course examines the role of artificial intelligence in organizational transformation and change. Students will explore how AI technologies affect strategy, operations, and decision-making processes while also considering ethical, managerial, and human factors. The course combines theoretical foundations with practical applications to prepare students to manage AI-enabled transformations effectively.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamental concepts and technologies of AI in business and management contexts.
Analyze the effects of AI on organizational processes and strategies.
Apply change management principles in AI-driven transformations.
Identify challenges and risks in AI adoption, considering ethical, social, and operational dimensions.
Develop practical solutions for implementing AI initiatives in organizations.

Hafta 2: AI Technologies and Capabilities (machine learning, natural language processing, robotics, and automation)
Hafta 3: AI in Strategic Management (aligning AI initiatives with organizational strategy)
Hafta 4: Organizational Change and Transformation (Kotter, ADKAR, Lewin)
Hafta 5: Challenges and Barriers to AI Adoption (resistance to change, skill gaps, technology-related stress, and infrastructure requirements)
Hafta 7: Data Management and AI Ethics (data quality, privacy, bias, and responsible AI practices)
Hafta 8: AI-Enabled Decision Making (augmented intelligence, AI use in operational and strategic decisions)
Hafta 9: AI Implementation Planning (project management, stakeholder engagement, and risk assessment)
Hafta 10: Human Factors in AI Transformation (employee engagement, training, and cultural change)
Hafta 11: AI in Digital Transformation (integration with other technologies: IoT, cloud, analytics)

courses.name: AI and Transformation Management
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip participants with the knowledge and tools to understand how technology transforms industries, societies, and individuals. It also seeks to develop the ability to identify opportunities and address challenges in technology-driven change processes. The course encourages critical evaluation of the ethical, social, and political implications of artificial intelligence.
courses.description: This course examines technological transformation from the Industrial Revolutions to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and addresses the fundamental concepts of transformation management. Students will analyze the role of AI in different disciplines and discuss its social, political, and ethical impacts. In addition, invited speakers from various sectors will share real-world experiences about the influence of AI and provide practical perspectives on technology-driven change.
learning_outcomes: Understand historical and contemporary technological transformations.
Identify the fundamental principles of transformation management in technology-driven change.
Evaluate the opportunities and challenges that artificial intelligence offers in different sectors.
Critically analyze the business, social, ethical, and political impacts of artificial intelligence.
Apply transformation management strategies to real-world scenarios.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the AI and Transformation Management course
Hafta 3: Forces and Challenges of Technological Transformation

Analyze the functions of the political system
Recognize the different political ideologies and political consequences of different political institutions and actors
Critically evaluate political arguments and processes
Develop citizenship awareness and democratic participation skills

courses.name: Communicating Sustainability: Building a Better Future
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand the role of communication and marketing in achieving sustainability goals, develop effective and ethical sustainability communication strategies, analyze global sustainability campaigns, identify greenwashing practices in advertising and brand communication, examine industry-led initiatives such as Ad Net Zero, design a sustainability-focused campaign, and apply crisis communication strategies in sustainability-related brand crises.
courses.description: This course examines topics at the intersection of communication, marketing, and sustainability, focusing on how brands and organizations can create ethical, transparent, and effective sustainability communication strategies. It combines marketing fundamentals, sustainability principles, crisis communication, and industry best practices to provide students with a strategic perspective on responsible advertising and brand management. Key topics include integrated marketing communication (IMC), sustainability communication, greenwashing, sectoral initiatives such as Ad Net Zero, crisis communication and reputation management, ethical marketing and consumer trust, and applied project development for a sustainability-focused communication plan. The course uses interactive discussions, real case analyses, and project-based learning to provide both theoretical knowledge and practical experience.
learning_outcomes: Critically evaluate sustainability-themed campaigns.
Identify false or misleading sustainability claims (greenwashing).
Develop responsible communication strategies by applying ethical advertising principles.

courses.name: CapitArt-X Media Art Lab
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students develop an interdisciplinary understanding of digital art, artificial intelligence, and media aesthetics. Students will strengthen their creative production, curatorial thinking, and critical media practice skills so they can actively participate in contemporary digital art environments.
courses.description: This course addresses digital art, artificial intelligence, and media aesthetics from an interdisciplinary perspective by combining theoretical foundations with creative production. Students begin by examining the historical development and core concepts of digital art, then explore the effects of artificial intelligence on artistic production and aesthetics. Throughout the course, they carry out practical work involving curatorial thinking, creative coding, data visualization, and interactive media applications, while also discussing the ethical and social dimensions of art and technology. Midterm and final projects provide opportunities to produce and exhibit original digital artworks. The course also focuses on virtual and augmented reality, exhibition design strategies, and art-technology integration to develop both critical and creative capacities.
learning_outcomes: Students will gain theoretical and practical knowledge in digital art and artificial intelligence.
Students will develop curatorial thinking and exhibition design skills.
Students will develop creative projects and take an active role in the field of digital art.

courses.name: Women in Games & We are in Games!
courses.purpose: This course critically examines the roles, representations, and participation of women and gender-diverse individuals in the game industry. Students explore structural inequalities in the sector, analyze character representations in games, learn inclusive design practices, and evaluate professional development pathways for women and marginalized communities.
courses.description: This course addresses gender representation, equality, and inclusivity in the game industry from an interdisciplinary perspective. Students examine the historical and contemporary roles of women and gender-diverse individuals in games, critically analyze narrative and visual representations in character design, and evaluate stereotypes and biases embedded in the industry. The course also covers inclusive game development practices, accessibility, labor conditions, workplace discrimination, and the contributions of women in both mainstream and independent games, along with community building, mentorship, and advocacy networks.
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to critically analyze gender representations in video games, including character design, narratives, and player roles.
Students will be able to identify structural barriers faced by women and gender-diverse individuals in the game industry and propose inclusive strategies to address them.
Students will be able to develop and present an original project (creative or analytical) that reflects a gender-focused perspective on game culture or design.

Hafta 8: Inequality and Labor Conditions in the Workplace
Hafta 9: Queer and Feminist Independent Games
Hafta 11: Mentorship and Networking in the Industry

courses.name: E Commerce
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the technological, social, and functional dimensions of electronic commerce and provide students with a comprehensive understanding of business models, marketing strategies, consumer behavior, and the ethical and legal aspects of e-commerce in the digital economy. The course also aims to develop students' strategic thinking skills in the field of digital commerce through current examples and case studies.
courses.description: Definition, scope, and development of electronic commerce. Digital business models and online value creation strategies. E-commerce infrastructure: hardware, software, internet, and mobile systems. Electronic payment systems and digital security. Digital marketing: SEO, SEM, social media, and email marketing. Online consumer behavior and customer relationship management (CRM). Subscription-based business models and user-generated content (UGC). Ethical, legal, and social aspects of e-commerce. B2B, B2C, C2C, and C2B e-commerce models. Mobile commerce (m-commerce) and next-generation applications. International e-commerce and cross-border digital marketing strategies. Discussions through case analyses and current examples.
learning_outcomes: Defines the basic components, business models, and current trends of e-commerce.
Analyzes e-commerce business models and strategies.
Explains e-commerce marketing and online consumer behavior.
Discusses legal, security, and ethical issues in e-commerce.
Applies digital marketing strategies (SEO, SEM, social media marketing).
Evaluates the social, mobile, and local marketing dimensions of e-commerce.

courses.name: Brand Management
courses.purpose: This course aims to teach students the fundamental concepts, strategies, and applications of branding. It also seeks to develop the analytical and decision-making skills needed for effective brand management by covering topics such as brand identity, brand positioning, brand equity, brand communication, and consumer perception. In addition, students will gain an understanding of brand-building processes through national and international examples.
courses.description: Branding and the marketing mix, building strong brands, marketing and communication programs, measuring brand performance, brand auditing, brand design and structures, managing brand equity for long-term success.

Understand branding and relate concepts with example cases

courses.name: Scientific community and communication
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students with the knowledge and skills needed to understand, analyze, and apply communication within the scientific community and between science and society. It also addresses the ethical and social dimensions of science communication while ensuring that scientific knowledge is conveyed to the public in an accessible, accurate, understandable, and effective way.
courses.description: This course examines the dynamics of communication within the scientific community and between science, media, and society. Students will learn the historical development of science communication, the roles of key actors (scientists, media, institutions, and religious organizations), and the functions of science communication in everyday life and during crises. In addition to the theoretical framework, the course includes practical work such as science journalism, storytelling, interviews, social media content production, and crisis communication.
learning_outcomes: Explain the definition, history, and main purposes of science communication.
Analyze the key actors involved in science communication, including scientists, media, the public, and religious institutions.
Evaluate the role of science communication during crises such as pandemics and disasters, and analyze case studies.
Compare the ways scientific information is conveyed in mass media such as TV, the internet, and social media.
Simplify scientific content for target audiences and present it through news, interviews, and storytelling techniques.
Transform scientific information into effective content for digital platforms such as Instagram Reels, TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and infographics.

courses.description: The course introduces AI from fundamental concepts to current applications such as Generative AI, and prepares students for the future AI world through ethics, practical tools, and strategic transformation topics.

Develop awareness of the social, ethical, and legal dimensions of artificial intelligence.
Create a prototype using two designated no-code tools, one visual and one text or generative AI based.

courses.name: Work and Employment in the 21st Century
courses.purpose: The course aims to enable students to analyze the economic, technological, and social transformations in modern working life. Students gain an analytical perspective on algorithmic management, digital labor, the future of employment, identity-based discrimination, care economy, and sustainable work models.
courses.description: This course examines the rapidly transforming world of work in the 21st century from a multidimensional perspective. Its main themes include digitalization, artificial intelligence, automation, platform economy, precarity, flexibility, migration, gender, care work, sustainability, labor rights, well-being, diversity, and the structural transformation of global production networks. Cross-cutting issues such as gender, identity, migration, climate crisis, and digital transformation are integrated throughout the course.
learning_outcomes: Explain the fundamental concepts of work and employment in the 21st century.
Analyze the effects of digitalization, artificial intelligence, and automation on the labor force.
Interpret the role of gender, identity, migration, and care work in the labor market.
Assess models of precarity, flexibility, and the platform economy.
Explain inequalities in working life and rights-based approaches.
Understand the concepts of sustainable work and just transition.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the concept of work in the 21st century
Hafta 3: Platform economy and gig work
Hafta 4: Gender, identity, and the labor force
Hafta 9: Workers' rights in the digital age
Hafta 10: Well-being in the workplace, mental health
Hafta 11: Sustainable work and green transition

courses.name: Financial Technologies
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamentals of fintech, the basic digital tools used in financial markets, and future developments in digital technology.
courses.description: This course covers financial technologies designed to enhance and automate the provision and utilization of financial services. It reviews technologies such as blockchain, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, and machine learning, along with their applications in financial markets. The course also addresses crowdfunding, insurtech, regtech, new payment architectures, upcoming developments in digital finance, and sustainability in the finance industry.

Analyze trends in financial technologies.
Interpret and predict possible future trends in digital finance.

courses.name: Globalization and Media
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic relationship between globalization and media, focusing on how media systems, industries, and technologies shape and are shaped by global cultural, political, and economic processes. Through theoretical perspectives and case studies, the course encourages students to critically engage with debates on media imperialism, cultural homogenization, hybridity, and glocalization, while also exploring the role of digital platforms, global news flows, and entertainment industries. By the end of the course, students will be able to evaluate the opportunities and challenges global media presents for identity, democracy, and cultural diversity, and apply this knowledge to contemporary media practices.
courses.description: This course examines the complex relationship between globalization and media. It addresses how media systems, technologies, and content shape and are shaped by global economic, political, and cultural processes. Students will engage with key theories, debates, and case studies to critically assess topics such as media imperialism, global media flows, cultural hybridity, and the role of digital platforms in the global age.
learning_outcomes: Have knowledge about the main definitions and debates of globalization
Have a critical understanding of the history of media in the age of globalization
Understand different approaches to cultural globalization and its relationship with new media
Develop skills in global media and communication
